label: Divrei HaYamim Aleph (1 Chronicles) — Part 2 — Chapters 10–29
summary_plain: Divrei HaYamim Aleph chapters 10 through 29 retell Dawid's reign from the death of Sha'ul to Dawid's final charge to Shelomoh. Dawid captures Yerushalayim and brings the Ark of the Covenant into the city with singing and dancing. He desires to build a house for YHWH, but YHWH says: Your son will build the house — I will establish his throne forever. Dawid prepares the materials for the temple, organizes the Levitical musicians and gatekeepers, and charges Shelomoh: Be strong and courageous. Know the Creator of your father and serve him with a whole heart.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from DSS fragments (4QChr) and the Orit Ge'ez. The Chronicler retells Dawid's reign with a distinctive emphasis: the military and political failures recorded in Shemu'el (Bat-Sheva, Amnon, Avshalom's revolt) are largely omitted. The Chronicler focuses on Dawid's role as temple preparer, worship organizer, and covenant transmitter. This is not historical falsification — it is theological selection. The Chronicler writes for the post-exilic community that needs to know its liturgical and institutional origins. Dawid's organization of the Levitical musicians (chapters 25-26) establishes the worship infrastructure that produced the Tehillim. The Chronicler's Dawid is the singer, the worshipper, the one who prepared everything so that YHWH's house could be built. The covenant promise in chapter 17 parallels 2 Shemu'el 7 — YHWH's unconditional oath to Dawid's line.

key_terms.definition: King of Yisra'EL, son of Yishai, of the tribe of Yehudah. In the Chronicler's account, Dawid is primarily the temple preparer and worship organizer — the one who set everything in place for the house of YHWH. Anglicized as David.

The sacred chest containing the tablets of the covenant. Dawid brings it into Yerushalayim with singing and dancing (chapter 15-16). The Ark is YHWH's throne on earth — the meeting point between YHWH and the covenant people.

1 Chronicles 17:12-14: YHWH promises Dawid an eternal throne — 'I will establish his throne forever. I will be his father, and he shall be my son.' This is the unconditional Dawid covenant — YHWH's oath that does not depend on human performance.

fill_blank.prompt: He shall build a house for me, and I will establish his ______ forever.
fill_blank.source_quote: He shall build a house for me, and I will establish his throne forever.

fill_blank.prompt: Know the Creator of your father and serve him with a whole heart and with a willing ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: And you, Shelomoh my son, know the Creator of your father and serve him with a whole heart and with a willing mind, for YHWH searches all hearts and understands every plan and thought.

fill_blank.prompt: Yours, O YHWH, is the greatness and the power and the glory and the victory and the ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: Yours, O YHWH, is the greatness and the power and the glory and the victory and the majesty, for all that is in the heavens and in the earth is yours.

multiple_choice.question: What does YHWH tell Dawid about building the temple?
options: You shall not build the house — your son shall build it, and I will establish his throne forever
multiple_choice.source_quote: You shall not build a house for me to dwell in... your son shall build a house for me, and I will establish his throne forever.

multiple_choice.question: What does Dawid organize for the temple worship?
options: The Levitical musicians and singers — setting up the worship infrastructure
multiple_choice.source_quote: Dawid also commanded the chiefs of the Levites to appoint their brothers as the singers who should play loudly on musical instruments.

multiple_choice.question: What does Dawid's final prayer declare?
options: That he is the greatest king
Yours, O YHWH, is the greatness and the power and the glory — everything in heaven and earth is yours
That Shelomoh should conquer all nations
multiple_choice.source_quote: Yours, O YHWH, is the greatness and the power and the glory and the victory and the majesty, for all that is in the heavens and in the earth is yours.

faq.question: Why does the Chronicler omit Dawid's failures that are recorded in Shemu'el?
faq.answer: The books of Shemu'el record Dawid's sin with Bat-Sheva, the rape of Tamar by Amnon, Avshalom's revolt, and other failures in detail. The Chronicler largely omits these episodes. This is not because the Chronicler denies they happened — it is because the Chronicler writes for a different audience with a different purpose. The post-exilic community in Yerushalayim needs to know its liturgical origins, its temple foundations, its worship structure. The Chronicler presents Dawid as the temple preparer, the worship organizer, the covenant transmitter. Both portraits are true. Shemu'el tells the whole human story. The Chronicler tells the covenant-institutional story. The Great Eraser mechanism sometimes uses selective citation in the same way — presenting only part of a text to serve a theological agenda. The difference is that both Shemu'el and Chronicles are in the canon. The reader has access to both. Selective erasure becomes a problem only when the other source is suppressed.

faq.question: What does Dawid's charge to Shelomoh reveal about covenant transmission?
faq.answer: 1 Chronicles 28:9: 'Know the Creator of your father and serve him with a whole heart and with a willing mind, for YHWH searches all hearts and understands every plan and thought.' Dawid does not tell Shelomoh to follow a priest, obey an institution, or accept a creed. He says: know The Creator directly. Serve with a whole heart. YHWH searches hearts — not credentials, not affiliations, not institutional memberships. This father-to-son transmission of covenant identity is the original mode. Before there were rabbis, before there were churches, before there were academies — there was a father telling his son: know YHWH. The Great Eraser replaces this direct transmission with institutional mediation. Dawid's charge says: the relationship is direct.
